title: Te Whakamarumaru o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku: Protecting Our River Ecosystem
subject: Science
culturalContext: This unit is centered around Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. Students will explore the traditional pūrākau (story) of Hinepūtehue, the guardian spirit of the awa, and investigate how mātauranga Māori and Western science can work together to address modern environmental challenges affecting our local waterway, including sedimentation from nearby farming and urban runoff.
learningObjectives: Analyze water quality data from Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku using scientific methods and compare findings to historical mātauranga Māori about the health of the awa.
Design and propose a culturally-informed restoration plan for a section of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku that integrates scientific data and mātauranga Māori principles like kaitiakitanga.
Communicate scientific findings and restoration proposals effectively to a community audience, using both scientific terminology and te reo Māori concepts.

activities.title: Whakatau & Whakapapa o Te Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge Hinepūtehue. A local kaumātua shares the pūrākau of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students create a collaborative 'whakapapa' (genealogy) map of the awa, identifying its source, tributaries, and the land uses within its catchment.

activities.title: He Tātari Wai - Water Quality Testing
activities.description: At a designated, safe site along the awa, students work in small groups to collect water samples. They use testing kits to gather scientific data on pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and macroinvertebrate diversity. They record their methods and data with precision.

activities.title: Te Hōpara Pūtaiao - The Science Explorer
activities.description: Back in the lab, students analyze their collected data, creating graphs and identifying trends. They compare their modern data with historical accounts and mātauranga (e.g., traditional indicators of healthy water like the presence of certain fish or plants) to assess the long-term health of the awa.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Hou - Designing a Restoration Plan
activities.description: Using their analysis, student groups design a detailed restoration plan for their tested section of the awa. Their plan must propose specific, evidence-based actions (e.g., planting native riparian vegetation to reduce sedimentation) and explain how these actions uphold the principle of kaitiakitanga (guardianship).

activities.title: Te Whakawhiti Kōrero - Community Presentation
activities.description: Groups present their findings and restoration proposals in a simulated community hui format. They must communicate their scientific evidence clearly and articulate how their plan respects and incorporates mātauranga Māori to protect the mauri (life force) of the awa.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council - Rivers and Streams Water Quality Data Portal
Local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi with knowledge of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
LaMotte Water Monitoring Kits or similar for freshwater testing
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) guide and resources

tasks: A detailed scientific report presenting water quality data from Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, including graphs and analysis.
A written proposal for a culturally-responsive restoration plan that integrates scientific findings with the principle of kaitiakitanga.
A 5-minute oral presentation of the proposal for a community audience.

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science; Investigating in science; Communicating in science
Living World: Ecology: Investigate the interdependence of living things in an ecosystem.
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems: Investigate the external and internal processes that shape and change the surface features of New Zealand.
